Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 3 of 2018 (Summonses Nos 9 and 22 of 2023)
Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Baker, Michael A (executor of the estate of Chantal Burnison, deceased)

Facts.Text: On 2017-11-20, Michael A Baker, as executor of the estate of Dr Chantal Burnison, commenced proceedings in HC/S 1070/2017 against BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd, Marcus Weber and Renslade Holdings Ltd.
Before 2017, Michael A Baker acted as executor of the estate of Dr Chantal Burnison, who had been involved in the development of the Ethocyn compound.

On 2019-08-08, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed proceedings in the United States District Court for the Central District of California against Michael A Baker and BCS Pharma Corporation concerning the contractual relationship between BCS and Nu Skin International.

On 2020-06-30, the Californian proceedings between BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd, Michael A Baker and BCS Pharma Corporation were stayed by agreement pending the outcome of appeal CA/CA 76/2020.
On 2021-03-26, the stay on the Californian proceedings involving BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Michael A Baker was lifted.
On 2021-06-16, Michael A Baker filed SIC/SUM 37/2021 seeking an anti-suit injunction to restrain prosecution of the Californian proceedings commenced by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the continuation of specified claims in the Californian proceedings by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber, after the grant of an anti-suit injunction, should result in civil contempt sanctions and whether any additional relief such as director disqualification is available.
Party_Details.Rule: Civil contempt for intentional disobedience of a court order arises where a person, with knowledge of the order, intentionally acts in a way that is inconsistent with the terms of that order. Under the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act 2016, a person who intentionally disobeys a court order may be punished by a fine and/or imprisonment. A director of a company that engages in such conduct may also face consequences under company legislation if the statutory criteria for disqualification based on conviction of specified offences are satisfied.
Party_Details.Application: Michael A Baker obtained an anti-suit injunction restraining the prosecution of certain categories of claims in the Californian proceedings. After the injunction was granted and after the dismissal of the appeal against that injunction, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd did not remove the specified claims from the Californian proceedings and continued to pursue them. Marcus Weber, as the sole shareholder and later sole director of BCS, instructed United States counsel in relation to the conduct of those proceedings. These steps were taken after the parties and their advisers were aware of the terms of the injunction and of the appellate court's decision upholding it. The court compared this conduct with the statutory framework for contempt sanctions and with the statutory wording governing director disqualification to determine the appropriate remedies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber committed civil contempt under s 4(1)(a) of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act 2016 by intentionally disobeying the anti-suit injunction issued on 2021-11-19. The court found that BCS and Weber knew of the injunction, as they had appealed against it, and that they continued to prosecute the Injuncted Claims in the Californian proceedings, including by retaining those claims in the Third Amended Complaint filed on 2022-04-07. The court noted that after the Court of Appeal dismissed CA/CA 70/2021 on 2022-09-21 and expressly stated that the defendants were to take steps to withdraw the claims within the ambit of the anti-suit injunction, BCS and Weber did not comply. Instead, BCS sought leave on 2023-01-25 to file a Fourth Amended Complaint and acknowledged in its memorandum that it had not complied with the Singapore injunction and that full compliance would require surrendering rights it wished to pursue. The court treated this as an express indication that BCS would not comply with the injunction. In assessing punishment, the court considered that breaches of anti-suit injunctions are analogous in seriousness to breaches of freezing orders because both orders protect the effectiveness of the forum court's processes and the fruits of litigation. The court reviewed English authorities such as Mobile Telecommunications Co KSC v HRH Prince Hussam Bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and Dell Emerging Markets (EMEA) Ltd v Systems Equipment Telecommunications Services SAL, and local authorities including Sembcorp Marine Ltd v Aurol Anthony Sabastian, Technigroup Far East Pte Ltd v Jaswinderpal Singh, OCM Opportunities Fund II, LP v Burhan Uray, BTS Tankers Pte Ltd v Energy & Commodity Pte Ltd, Mok Kah Hong v Zheng Zhuan Yao, PT Sandipala Arthaputra v STMicroelectronics Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, and WestBridge Ventures II Investment Holdings v Anupam Mittal. The court concluded that the contempt in this case was more serious than in WestBridge because Weber was ordinarily resident in Singapore, the contemnors took active steps in the foreign court including applications for a US anti-suit injunction and a temporary restraining order aimed at preventing enforcement of the Singapore anti-suit injunction and the contempt proceedings, the anti-suit injunction protected a final and unappealable judgment in Suit 3, and the contemnors persisted in disobedience even after their appeal against the anti-suit injunction was dismissed. The court also found that Weber procured BCS to act in contempt, including by replacing Hartono Sianto as director after Sianto indicated that the Injuncted Claims should be withdrawn. Despite these aggravating factors, the court decided not to impose imprisonment on Weber because the Californian court had not granted any relief undermining the Singapore judgment or the anti-suit injunction; the foreign applications for a temporary restraining order and a US anti-suit injunction were dismissed and the Injuncted Claims remained pending. The court therefore imposed a fine of SGD 80,000 on BCS and the maximum fine of SGD 100,000 on Weber, without suspension. On the application for a director disqualification order under s 154 of the Companies Act, the court analysed whether a finding of civil contempt under s 4(1) of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act constitutes a conviction of an offence for the purposes of s 154(2)(a). The court considered the definition of 'offence' in the Criminal Procedure Code, the structure of s 154 of the Companies Act, the distinction between criminal and civil contempt in cases such as Tan Beow Hiong v Tan Boon Aik, Maruti Shipping Pte Ltd v Tay Sien Djim, Li Shengwu v Attorney-General, and Tan Liang Joo John v Attorney-General, and the provisions of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act concerning investigations, waiver of contempt, and the role of the Attorney-General. The court concluded that civil contempt by disobedience under s 4(1) is not an 'offence' for the purposes of s 154 of the Companies Act and that a finding of such contempt is not a 'conviction' within that section. Accordingly, the court dismissed SUM 22 and declined to make a director disqualification order against Weber. As to Hartono Sianto, the court examined s 6(2) of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act and the evidence of his conduct as a director of BCS. The court accepted that Sianto became aware of the anti-suit injunction and, upon receiving the 2022-09-28 letter with the order and penal notice, forwarded it to Weber and BCS's US lawyers. The court also accepted documentary evidence that on 2023-05-02 Sianto told Weber that BCS should withdraw the claims prohibited by the anti-suit injunction and that he would resign if this was not done, and that Weber responded by asking him to resign and be replaced by Weber as director, which occurred on 2023-05-03. The court held that Baker had not proved beyond reasonable doubt that Sianto failed to take all reasonable steps to prevent or stop BCS's contempt, or that he consented to, connived in, or was otherwise knowingly concerned in the contempt within s 6(2)(b). The contempt application against Sianto was therefore dismissed. The court directed that, if parties could not agree on costs, they were to file written submissions on costs within 21 days, limited to ten pages excluding any schedule of disbursements.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 3 of 2018 (Summonses Nos 9 and 22 of 2023)
Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 2019-03-05, Hartono Sianto became a director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd.
On 2019-09-30, Marcus Weber ceased to be a director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and was replaced by Chuah Lay San as a director together with Hartono Sianto.
On 2022-09-21, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd had two directors, Hartono Sianto and Chuah Lay San.
On 2022-09-21, the Court of Appeal dismissed CA/CA 70/2021, which was BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd's appeal against the anti-suit injunction judgment.

On 2019-08-08, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd commenced proceedings in the United States District Court for the Central District of California against Michael A Baker and BCS Pharma Corporation concerning the contractual relationship between BCS and Nu Skin International.
On 2019-08-08, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed an Initial Complaint in the Californian proceedings setting out its claims against Michael A Baker and BCS Pharma Corporation.
On 2021-04-27, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed a First Amended Complaint in the Californian proceedings.
On 2021-08-27, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed a Second Amended Complaint in the Californian proceedings.

On 2021-11-19, the Singapore International Commercial Court issued an anti-suit injunction order directed at BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and related parties concerning specified categories of claims in the Californian proceedings.
On 2022-04-07, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed a Third Amended Complaint in the Californian proceedings after the anti-suit injunction had been issued.
On 2022-09-22, lawyers for Michael A Baker sent a demand to BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd requesting compliance with the anti-suit injunction following the dismissal of CA/CA 70/2021.

On 2022-09-28, lawyers for Michael A Baker sent a letter with a copy of the anti-suit injunction and a penal notice to Hartono Sianto as a director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd, stating that BCS had not withdrawn the specified claims from the Californian proceedings and demanding compliance by 2022-10-05.

On 2023-01-25, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed a motion in the Californian court seeking leave to file a Fourth Amended Complaint and lodged a memorandum stating that it had not fully complied with the Singapore anti-suit injunction and that full compliance would affect claims it wished to pursue.
On 2023-02-07, Michael A Baker filed a Statement of Committal in Singapore setting out contempt by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber in relation to the anti-suit injunction.
On 2023-03-13, lawyers for Michael A Baker sent a letter to Hartono Sianto enclosing the contempt application SIC/SUM 9/2023 and related documents.

On 2023-07-14, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed an application in the Californian court seeking an anti-suit injunction to restrain Michael A Baker from prosecuting the Singapore contempt application or enforcing the Singapore anti-suit injunction.

On 2023-07-24, BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd filed an ex parte application in the Californian court for a temporary restraining order in terms similar to its application for a United States anti-suit injunction.
On 2023-07-26, the Californian court dismissed the ex parte application by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd for a temporary restraining order.

On 2023-08-11, the Californian court heard the application by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd for a United States anti-suit injunction relating to the Singapore anti-suit injunction and contempt proceedings.
On 2023-09-05, the Californian court dismissed the application by BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd for a United States anti-suit injunction.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd, as a corporate party an anti-suit injunction, engaged in conduct amounting to civil contempt by continuing to pursue specified claims in foreign proceedings after the injunction and appellate decisions, and what sanctions should be imposed.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporation commits civil contempt when, with knowledge of a subsisting court order, it intentionally acts in a manner inconsistent with that order, including by continuing foreign proceedings that the order restrains. Under the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act 2016, such conduct may be sanctioned by fines up to the statutory maximum, and the court may consider aggravating and mitigating factors such as persistence, impact on the opposing party, and steps taken to comply.
Party_Details.Application: BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd was the claimant in the Californian proceedings and was expressly named in the anti-suit injunction issued on 2021-11-19. After the injunction and after the dismissal of the appeal CA/CA 70/2021 on 2022-09-21, BCS did not remove the specified categories of claims from the Californian proceedings. Instead, it filed a Third Amended Complaint on 2022-04-07 that retained those claims and later sought leave on 2023-01-25 to file a Fourth Amended Complaint while acknowledging that it had not fully complied with the Singapore injunction. BCS also initiated applications in the Californian court on 2023-07-14 and 2023-07-24 seeking orders to restrain enforcement of the Singapore anti-suit injunction and the Singapore contempt proceedings. These actions were taken after BCS and its advisers were aware of the terms of the injunction and the appellate court's directions, and they formed the factual basis for the court's assessment of contempt and the level of monetary sanction.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd intentionally disobeyed the anti-suit injunction and therefore committed civil contempt under s 4(1)(a) of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act 2016. The court relied on the fact that BCS had appealed the anti-suit injunction, demonstrating knowledge of it, and that the Court of Appeal in CA/CA 70/2021 dismissed the appeal and directed the defendants to withdraw claims within the ambit of the injunction. Despite this, BCS retained the Injuncted Claims in the Third Amended Complaint filed on 2022-04-07 and did not take steps to withdraw them. The court placed weight on BCS's memorandum filed on 2023-01-25 in support of its motion for leave to file a Fourth Amended Complaint, where BCS stated that full compliance with the Singapore injunction would require it to give up claims that depended on contesting the Singapore orders' conclusion about the existence of a trust. The court interpreted this as an explicit acknowledgment of non-compliance and an intention not to comply. In assessing punishment, the court considered the seriousness of breaches of anti-suit injunctions, drawing analogies to breaches of freezing orders as discussed in English cases such as Mobile Telecommunications Co KSC v HRH Prince Hussam Bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and Dell Emerging Markets (EMEA) Ltd v Systems Equipment Telecommunications Services SAL. The court also considered local authorities on contempt sentencing, including Sembcorp Marine Ltd v Aurol Anthony Sabastian, Technigroup Far East Pte Ltd v Jaswinderpal Singh, OCM Opportunities Fund II, LP v Burhan Uray, BTS Tankers Pte Ltd v Energy & Commodity Pte Ltd, Mok Kah Hong v Zheng Zhuan Yao, PT Sandipala Arthaputra v STMicroelectronics Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, and WestBridge Ventures II Investment Holdings v Anupam Mittal. The court found several aggravating factors: the breach was deliberate and continuous over approximately two years; BCS took active steps in the Californian court to seek a United States anti-suit injunction and a temporary restraining order aimed at preventing enforcement of the Singapore anti-suit injunction and the contempt proceedings; the anti-suit injunction protected a final and unappealable judgment in Suit 3; and BCS persisted in its conduct despite the dismissal of its appeal against the anti-suit injunction and the Court of Appeal's express direction to withdraw the Injuncted Claims. The court also considered the prejudice to Michael A Baker and the other protected parties, including the time, expense, and ongoing risk associated with the foreign proceedings. Balancing these factors and comparing the case with WestBridge, the court concluded that a higher fine than the SGD 70,000 imposed in WestBridge was justified and imposed a fine of SGD 80,000 on BCS. The court declined to suspend the fine, noting the prolonged and deliberate nature of the non-compliance and the absence of any indication that BCS would comply if given further time.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 3 of 2018 (Summonses Nos 9 and 22 of 2023)
Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Marcus Weber

Facts.Text: By 2003-01-01, Marcus Weber had become a permanent resident of Singapore after obtaining permission to work in Singapore in 2002.
Before 2017, Marcus Weber was the sole shareholder and sole director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd.
Before 2017, Marcus Weber arranged for BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd to receive payments from Nu Skin International under a supply and distribution agreement relating to Ethocyn.
Before 2017, Marcus Weber used companies including BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Renslade Holdings Ltd in connection with income generated from the Ethocyn Rights.

On 2019-08-08, Marcus Weber, through BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd, initiated proceedings in the United States District Court for the Central District of California against Michael A Baker and BCS Pharma Corporation concerning the contractual relationship between BCS and Nu Skin International.
On 2019-09-30, Marcus Weber ceased to be a director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd while remaining its sole shareholder.
On 2023-05-03, Marcus Weber became the sole director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd after the resignation of Hartono Sianto.

On 2021-11-19, an anti-suit injunction order was issued that expressly named Marcus Weber and referred to his capacity as director, officer, servant, agent or shareholder of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Renslade Holdings Ltd.
By 2022-01-01, Marcus Weber was instructing United States lawyers on behalf of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd in relation to the conduct of the Californian proceedings.

On 2023-05-02, Hartono Sianto sent an email to Marcus Weber stating that BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd should withdraw the claims in the Californian action that were prohibited under the anti-suit injunction and that he would resign as director if this was not done.

On 2023-05-02, Marcus Weber replied to Hartono Sianto by email, declining to agree to the withdrawal of the prohibited claims and asking Sianto to resign as director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and to replace him with Weber.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Marcus Weber, as the controlling individual behind BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and a person expressly named in the anti-suit injunction, engaged in conduct amounting to civil contempt by continuing the prosecution of specified foreign claims after the injunction and appellate decisions, and whether he should also be a director disqualification order.
Party_Details.Rule: An individual commits civil contempt when, with knowledge of a court order, the individual intentionally acts in a way that is inconsistent with that order, including by directing or procuring a company to continue foreign proceedings that the order restrains. Under the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act 2016, such conduct may be sanctioned by fines and imprisonment. Under s 154 of the Companies Act, a director may be disqualified if convicted of specified offences connected with the formation or management of a corporation, but civil contempt by disobedience is not treated as such an offence for the purposes of that section.
Party_Details.Application: Marcus Weber was the sole shareholder and, at different times, a director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd. The anti-suit injunction issued on 2021-11-19 expressly referred to him and to his roles in BCS and Renslade Holdings Ltd. After the injunction and after the dismissal of the appeal CA/CA 70/2021, BCS continued to pursue the specified categories of claims in the Californian proceedings. Evidence from Hartono Sianto indicated that Weber was the person instructing United States counsel on the conduct of those proceedings and that, when Sianto proposed withdrawing the prohibited claims on 2023-05-02, Weber responded by asking Sianto to resign and by taking over as sole director on 2023-05-03. These facts showed that Weber controlled BCS's response to the injunction and chose to maintain the foreign claims. The court compared this conduct with the statutory framework for contempt sanctions and with the wording of s 154 of the Companies Act, and concluded that monetary sanctions were appropriate for contempt but that the statutory conditions for a director disqualification order were not met because the civil contempt finding did not amount to a conviction of an offence within s 154.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Marcus Weber committed civil contempt by intentionally disobeying the anti-suit injunction. It accepted evidence that Weber was the owner and controller of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and that he instructed BCS's United States lawyers in the Californian proceedings. The anti-suit injunction expressly restrained Weber, whether acting personally or in his capacities connected with BCS and Renslade Holdings Ltd, from prosecuting or continuing to prosecute proceedings relating to the specified subject matter. The court noted that Weber appealed against the anti-suit injunction, demonstrating knowledge of it, and that the Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal in CA/CA 70/2021 and directed the defendants to withdraw the claims within the ambit of the injunction. Despite this, Weber caused BCS to retain the Injuncted Claims in the Third Amended Complaint and to seek leave to file a Fourth Amended Complaint while acknowledging non-compliance. The court also relied on the email exchange of 2023-05-02, in which Hartono Sianto stated that BCS should withdraw the prohibited claims and that he would resign if this was not done, and Weber responded by declining to withdraw the claims and asking Sianto to resign and replace him with Weber as director. The court treated this as evidence that Weber took over the directorship to continue the existing approach to the Californian proceedings. In determining sentence, the court applied the framework from Sembcorp Marine Ltd v Aurol Anthony Sabastian, focusing on whether a fine would be adequate to punish and deter the contempt. It considered the nature and gravity of the contempt, including the analogy between breaches of anti-suit injunctions and breaches of freezing orders discussed in English cases such as Mobile Telecommunications Co KSC v HRH Prince Hussam Bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and Dell Emerging Markets (EMEA) Ltd v Systems Equipment Telecommunications Services SAL, and local cases such as Technigroup Far East Pte Ltd v Jaswinderpal Singh and OCM Opportunities Fund II, LP v Burhan Uray. The court identified several aggravating factors: Weber was ordinarily resident in Singapore and had recently assumed the role of sole director of BCS; the contempt was deliberate and continuous; Weber procured BCS to act in contempt; BCS and Weber took active steps in the Californian court to seek a United States anti-suit injunction and a temporary restraining order aimed at preventing enforcement of the Singapore anti-suit injunction and the contempt proceedings; the anti-suit injunction protected a final and unappealable judgment in Suit 3; and Weber persisted in disobedience after the dismissal of the appeal against the anti-suit injunction and after the Court of Appeal's express direction to withdraw the Injuncted Claims. The court compared the case with WestBridge Ventures II Investment Holdings v Anupam Mittal and concluded that the contempt here was more serious. However, the court decided not to impose imprisonment because the Californian court had not granted any relief undermining the Singapore judgment or the anti-suit injunction; the applications for a temporary restraining order and a United States anti-suit injunction were dismissed and the Injuncted Claims remained pending. The court therefore imposed the maximum fine of SGD 100,000 on Weber and declined to suspend it. On the application for a director disqualification order under s 154 of the Companies Act, the court analysed whether a finding of civil contempt under s 4(1) of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act constitutes a conviction of an offence in connection with the formation or management of a corporation. The court examined the definition of 'offence' in the Criminal Procedure Code, the structure of s 154 of the Companies Act, the distinction between criminal and civil contempt in authorities such as Tan Beow Hiong v Tan Boon Aik, Maruti Shipping Pte Ltd v Tay Sien Djim, Li Shengwu v Attorney-General, and Tan Liang Joo John v Attorney-General, and the provisions of the Administration of Justice (Protection) Act on investigations, waiver of contempt, and the role of the Attorney-General. It concluded that civil contempt by disobedience under s 4(1) is not an 'offence' for the purposes of s 154 and that a finding of such contempt is not a 'conviction' within that section. The court therefore dismissed SUM 22 and declined to make a director disqualification order against Weber.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 3 of 2018 (Summonses Nos 9 and 22 of 2023)
Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Renslade Holdings Ltd

Facts.Text: Before 2017, Renslade Holdings Ltd, also referred to as Renslade (HK), was one of the companies used by Marcus Weber in connection with income generated from the Ethocyn Rights.
On 2020-01-11, Renslade Holdings Ltd paid the sum of US$10,330,658.91 into court in Singapore pursuant to order SIC/ORC 2/2020.

On 2021-11-19, an anti-suit injunction order was issued that expressly named Renslade Holdings Ltd and restrained it, together with BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber, from prosecuting or continuing to prosecute certain proceedings relating to the Ethocyn Rights.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Renslade Holdings Ltd, as a company associated with the Ethocyn-related arrangements and named in the anti-suit injunction, was any contempt findings or additional relief in the context of the continuation of foreign proceedings.
Party_Details.Rule: A company named in an anti-suit injunction may be for civil contempt if it intentionally acts inconsistently with the injunction after having knowledge of it. Liability and sanctions depend on evidence of such conduct and the statutory framework for contempt.
Party_Details.Application: Renslade Holdings Ltd was named in the anti-suit injunction alongside BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber. It had previously paid a specified sum into court in Singapore in early 2020. The judgment text for the contempt proceedings focuses on the conduct of BCS and Marcus Weber in relation to the continuation of the Californian proceedings and on the role of Hartono Sianto as a director of BCS. The text does not describe separate post-injunction conduct by Renslade Holdings Ltd in foreign proceedings that formed the basis of the contempt application. Accordingly, the court's analysis and sanctions were directed at BCS and Marcus Weber, and no separate contempt sanction was imposed on Renslade Holdings Ltd in this judgment.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court's contempt analysis and sanctions in this judgment were directed at BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd and Marcus Weber. Although Renslade Holdings Ltd was named in the anti-suit injunction and in the earlier Suit 3 judgment, the contempt application and the findings in this judgment did not identify separate acts by Renslade Holdings Ltd in relation to the continuation of the Californian proceedings. The court therefore did not make a specific contempt finding or impose a separate sanction on Renslade Holdings Ltd in this decision.
